TRAVAUX DE L'INSTITUT

Christian GUTLEBEN

Université des Sciences Humaines de Strasbourg

AN INTERVIEW WITH IRIS MURDOCH

### An Introduction

In contemporary English university fiction (the field of my research work), academics practice the art of self-derision. They jeer at the(ir) excess of theorizing, at the far-fetchedness of modem criticism, but beyond their mockery lies a deep academic crisis where the poststructuralist culture threatens the traditional values of liberal humanism.

Now, who could better talk about this crisis than academics themselves? They write the novels about academic life, they write the essays about cultural evolution: the discourse of academics constitutes the intellectual background of the literature in which they take part. It is for me a starting point for a reflection on contemporary fiction and criticism: it is also, in a way, the referent of the fictive texts which I am studying.

The unexpected interest of my interviews with academics was human. And this was particularly true with Iris Murdoch who very kindly accepted to help an academic novice like myself. I met her at home in North Oxford in the midst of pots of plants and stacks of books -sometimes one on top of the other; she appeared intellectually alert, ethically concerned, francophile but Derrida-phobe. That day we had a genuine conversation beside a fake fire-place.

170

CHRISTIAN GUTLEBEN

### December 3rd, 1990

According to you , Iris Murdoch, is the comic of Under The Net linked to its first person narration?

I think Under The Net is a freak. It was my first novel, it was dedicated to Raymond Queneau, and it was inspired by him and by Samuel Beckett. The funniness of Under The Net is special to it, Beckettish, Queneauesque. I've only written three or four first person novels and I don't think that they differ very much in this respect from the other novels. The thing about the novel (however sad) is that it is comic, it is basically a comic form.

This is indeed what you said in an interview with John Haffenden. What do you mean by 'comic form ' ?

That the traditional novel is a look at human life, it is about people, their worries, what makes them happy, what makes them sad, and so on. And human life depends very much on the comic. I mean that in all human life, even in terrible situations, people make jokes, and the funniness is something very profound. Funniness is also, I think, an aspect of freedom, something that totalitarian atmospheres can destroy. But ordinary free human life is absolutely full of funniness. You may want to distinguish between wit and the wild, bumptious sort of comic. I think that this is just natural to the traditional novel. It may be that in the post-Derrida novel, the funniness has been killed, together with other things.

Or very much mixed with tragedy anyway.

I wouldn't say mixed, I think tragedy is a heavy word. We'll turn to tragedy in a moment. I'm hostile to Derrida, I think that his influence has been damaging. He has been bad for young writers and young critics who are afraid to write in a traditional mode; they think they've got to make it into a « text » very dense and very poetic. This is an art form, but it is a difficult art form, and not many people, I think, can succeed at it; but this means that people who have a talent for story telling and could write a traditional novel are afraid to do so. I think anyway that Derrida has no philosophy, it is a pseudo-philosophy; archi-écriture is a form of determinism. There is a lot of truth in what he says (earlier stated by Wittgenstein), that words are not names of things, that words

AN INTERVIEW WITH IRIS MURDOCH

171

have meaning through connections with other words, all the things that Saussure was showing us. But he proceeds into a metaphysical land where he pictures a scene as one where (Hegel's influence is strong here, of course) reality is at a level of linguistic texture which is so dense or so extensive that the individual human person is not really conscious of what he can say, that it is language saying him, rather than he saying language. Your individual meaning is taken away, you are a rendez¬ vous of codes. Now, here again, there's a little bit of truth. Individuals are limited by their language. But this does not imply that there is a (postulated Hegelian) structure of language which alone is real and true.

On the other hand, you see, one might say to someone like Derrida, well, if one is the slave of determined linguistic codes, what happens to truth, what happens to what you're saying? Why should I believe what you are saying? Here comes the rather delicate moment when persons who follow Derrida would reply, well, there are some people who live at a higher level of sophistication, they are the « creative people », they are the people who can play with language, and this is the area of freedom, this is where we can change the world. Here Heidegger plays a role. Heidegger suggests that we can't use ordinary philosophical language any more, we have to poeticize language in order to make it true. Well, Derrida and his friends would be among these poeticisers, and they would hope that the young critics and writers would be out there too, in the jeu de signifiants. This has been a bad influence. People often can't be bothered to read Derrida, his rethoric is too arcane, they just pick up a few ideas, and then they are frightened to write except in this very constrained manner.

Derrida writes in « La Loi du Genre » that there is, within the law of genre, an anti-law that accounts for the impossibility of a pure genre and that explains its hybrid nature. Now this seems also relevant for comedy. Comedy cannot be pure comedy any more, it is fundamentally undermined by modern society, and it has to be a hybrid form; the novel is hybrid by nature: it is autobiography, romance, poetry, comedy, tragedy.

Well, you mean that everything is a fragmented genre.

Yes. Purity of genre does not exist any more.

Well, I don't agree. I think this is a very general danger to our civilization. Philosophy must not be fragmented into sociology,
